title: Economic Bulletin Issue 2, 2026
summary: At its March 19, 2026 meeting, the ECB Governing Council kept its three key ECB interest rates unchanged, maintaining its commitment to ensure inflation stabilises at the 2% target in the medium term. Staff macroeconomic projections show headline inflation revised up to 2.6% in 2026 (from December 2025 projections), while GDP growth was revised down to 0.9% in 2026, reflecting the disruptive impact of the Middle East war on commodity markets, real incomes, and confidence.
key_points: ECB Governing Council held rates unchanged at March 19, 2026 meeting
Headline inflation projected at 2.6% in 2026, 2.0% in 2027, 2.1% in 2028 – revised up from December 2025 projections due to higher energy prices from the war
Core inflation (excluding energy and food) projected at 2.3% in 2026, 2.2% in 2027, 2.1% in 2028
GDP growth revised down to 0.9% in 2026 (down 0.3pp), 1.3% in 2027 (down 0.1pp), and 1.4% in 2028 – unchanged
War in Middle East creating upside risks for inflation and downside risks for growth
Alternative scenarios show a prolonged oil/gas supply disruption would result in higher inflation and lower growth than baseline
why_it_matters: The ECB's unchanged stance with upgraded inflation forecasts and downgraded growth underscores the policy dilemma posed by the Middle East conflict. The Governing Council explicitly stated it is 'not pre-committing to a particular rate path' and will follow a data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach. Upcoming economic data releases will be critical for future rate decisions, as the ECB navigates upside inflation risks against downside growth risks.
macro_context: The ECB faces a stagflationary challenge driven by the Middle East war: energy-driven inflation upside combined with growth downside. Staff baseline projections are conditioned on futures prices for energy commodities as of the March 11 cut-off date. Alternative scenarios suggest a prolonged oil/gas supply disruption would result in higher inflation and lower growth than baseline. The Governing Council highlighted the urgent need to strengthen the euro area economy while maintaining sound public finances.
market_structure_context: The Economic Bulletin includes sections on financing conditions, credit developments, and liquidity conditions with monetary policy operations (November 2025 - February 2026). While the full source was truncated, these sections would contain relevant market structure content on euro area credit conditions and ECB operational framework.
